ground_truth.title: What do you pay attention to if you are going to buy a web hosting service?
ground_truth.author: HOSTLAR
ground_truth.main_content:
HOSTLAR
11-29-2024, 09:03 AM

Hello

What do you pay attention to if you are going to buy a web hosting service?

Is location important for you?

Regards.

1Ali
11-29-2024, 01:50 PM

Phone support.

fullarton
11-29-2024, 02:43 PM

Confidence that the chosen supplier will provide good service, minimum downtimes, good after sales support. Location is a very close second to that: I assess location on deemed "security" - I want to believe that GDPR will be observed and that data will not be sent around the planet.
Price is important, but certainly not the be-all-and-end-all.

domaincart
11-29-2024, 04:40 PM

Offering to choose multi location is a privilege for buyers because EU servers can be faster than US for most people. Effective support is another important thing. Also providing some services like free backup/restore, migration and hosting tools(like Jetbackup, Softaculous, Site Builder etc.) are good options. Plan features (enough disk space and cPanel limits), payment options(CC must be exist) and uptime and company reliability is other things.

jonburnaby
11-29-2024, 08:24 PM

Once I've selected a web host, I order it for a month or so, create a simple WordPress site on it, and do once a minute web monitoring that includes a required unique piece of text that must be on a page of the site to ensure that the web monitor does not get fooled by HTTP Codes of 200 where the Page is not being fully rendered. That gives me an idea of what I care about most: Reliability.

Uncensored-Hosting
11-30-2024, 02:25 PM

Response time via email/phone.

Quality of response/resolution.

Censorship/micromanagement

Scalability

Cost

Location is more important when you seek bulletproof hosting and/or need to be hosted somewhere with little or no legal ramification.

Phil McKerracher
11-30-2024, 04:19 PM

I completely agree. You can't tell anything from the marketing or reviews, you have to actually try them out.

I actually signed up with half a dozen different hosts last year to see which ones were best for me (and whether any were better than my existing host). Almost immediately I discovered that some of them were pretty hopeless - it was a real struggle to get a site online with them. I compared page load times and tested things like making and restoring a backup as well.

Regarding quality of support - ideally you shouldn't need to contact them. I'd prefer a host where servers are properly monitored and you can be confident that any problems will be fixed without having to raise a ticket and nag them.

Chaddy
12-01-2024, 04:23 AM

In terms of buying a hosting service, I would also say that after sales support is important, both by phone and ticket / email depending on provider.
In terms of other features such as server location, that depends more on the target audience for the website, as an example a website targeting mostly UK users should be hosted in the UK but one targeting Australian users should be hosted in Au, both for Laytency as the site will load quicker and for privacy and data compliance as well. If you think some content might have a world wide interest then that is where the use of a CDN, Content Delivery network comes in, plus they can also help with both privacy and security such as offering DDOS protection and site speed as well.

MechanicWeb-shoss
12-03-2024, 07:51 PM

Several key indicators hint at whether the respective provider would turn out to be good for you.

Shortlist providers based on the following:

1. Do they meet your requirements?

2. Is support 24/7?

3. Is the price too good to be true? Is it realistic?

4. Is the cost of 50 hosting accounts on their lowest plan equivalent to the price of a medium-sized recent-generation dedicated server? If the answer is no, are they incurring losses by providing you with hosting or overloading their servers to make a profit?

5. Transparency. How upfront do they communicate their hosting package limitations in the package description on their primary website, or do they tend to hide something? Providers that are upfront and transparent tend to provide good service.

6. For small to medium providers, run a whois check on their domain. If it is privacy protected, what is their reason for doing so other than hiding their address? It is not due to spam. A provider can handle that, or you would expect they should be able to handle that for at least their primary emails and phone numbers.

7. Easily available public uptime status page with historical uptime statistics managed by a third party. This will usually give you a good idea about their overall uptime.

8. Infrastructure details. Are the hardware and software specifications of their servers easily available on the website? Does it have detailed information on what type of CPU, memory, disk, uplink, etc., they are using? Do they highlight any specific features and explain why those will benefit you?

After you have shortlisted based on the above:

Get a taste of support firsthand. Contact the shortlisted providers using their support channel and ask a few questions. How and what they answer will give you the final hints on whether they might turn out to be a good provider for you.

Remember, a provider cannot be good for everyone. A provider who is good to most can turn out to be a nightmare to you.

ezhost100
12-07-2024, 11:37 AM

When picking a web hosting service, think of it like choosing the perfect home for a website. Things you need to pay attention to are, Speed, Reliability, Price, Customer support, Storage, Easy setup and Location. Location can be important. Servers closer to where most of your website visitors live can make your site load faster. For example, if most of your visitors are in the United States, a server in the US might be better than one in Europe.

ezhost100
12-07-2024, 11:49 AM

I agree as long as the host offers reasonable 1 month plans. For shared hosting most hosts offer 1 year contract at a discounted price but the month by month prices are way higher. Also most site offer 30 day refund if not satisfied that way you can do many hosts if you can afford. What you propose is great as long as you know what you are looking for. Most first time buyers don't have a clue. I agree with you 100% that reliability is one of the key components to look at when buying web hosting service.

jonburnaby
12-07-2024, 12:20 PM

Any host I know of is more than happy to convert your current month by month plan to an annual plan, so I pay monthly while I'm testing for Reliability, even if it costs me a little more. Worth it compared to how much trouble I've been through with some hosts trying to get a refund on an annual plan. The real issue is that many hosts do not offer any plan shorter than 12 months, or occasionally 6.

John Mo
12-09-2024, 08:25 AM

I pay attention to price, speed, uptime, server location, and customer support. Those are the key factors that make a real difference for me.

selinux
12-09-2024, 08:48 AM

I would pay attention to the age of the hardware, and what kind of network equipment and the setup, and how everything is structured.

MivoCloud
12-09-2024, 09:04 AM

Technical support should be very important in choosing a hosting company. You must be sure that if you contact us, you will receive a response as quickly as possible and that they will help you at any time of the day. It is also worth paying attention to the location where you can place the servers and whether the company has its own data center

HostXNow
12-09-2024, 10:13 AM

For me, I'd say

1. Uptime.
2. Anti-DDoS protection: There is no point in having the best of everything without DDoS protection.
2. Fastest page loading times: Not all providers use the same network/hardware/software/stack. Some are much better than others.
3. Within budget. It has to be affordable.
4. Support: If Uptime is excellent and pages load fast, then Support isn't needed much, if at all.
5. Server Location. However, if the network/hardware/software/stack is excellent, the location doesn't matter as much when serving websites. Server location is critical for large upload/download sharing sites or Gaming purposes.
6. Features: Free SSLs, Free Backups, Auto Script Installer (WPToolkit/Softaculous), LiteSpeed Web Server, Firewall/Malware protection (CSF/Imunify360), Email Filtering (MailChannels) so can send emails without issues.

As you can see, there's a lot to it. It can take time to have a better understanding of all those things.

Good luck.

Goviphosting
12-10-2024, 04:08 PM

Location is important depending on your site niche and your expectations.

1. No of Years in Business
2. Server Speed And Performence
3. Friendly customer support using Live chat, WhatsApp, Skype, Telegram, Tickets etc
4. Multi Location Hosting
5. SEO Friendly
6. Offering Different Hosting Niches under one roof

anotherdrz
12-11-2024, 07:43 PM

Two key things I learned I need: backups in a different location which I can access if the entire data center fails, and colocating in a reasonably good data center...
